title: The Substation Fire
brief: A transformer at the Ikeja North feeder substation explodes at 2:14 a.m., plunging three districts into darkness and costing the DisCo a fortune in a week when a privatisation audit is underway. The blast was no accident: a bypassed protection relay let the fault run unchecked. Someone inside the control room arranged it.

suspects.name: Engr. Tunde Bakare
suspects.motive: Passed over for the regional manager post given to a younger colleague; bitter and vocal about it.
suspects.alibi: Says he was asleep at home in Ogba; lives alone.
suspects.note: Loud, aggrieved, keeps steering talk to his grievance.

suspects.name: Chidinma Okafor
suspects.motive: Recently disciplined for an unauthorised relay test; could be hiding negligence.
suspects.alibi: On the logged night shift in the control room until 1:50 a.m., then signed out.
suspects.note: Precise, defensive about her competence.

suspects.name: Alhaji Musa Danjuma
suspects.motive: His firm's service contract was about to be cancelled by the audit; the fire keeps the DisCo dependent on him.
suspects.alibi: At a wedding in Abeokuta until late; many guests saw him.
suspects.note: Smooth, generous with names of witnesses.

suspects.name: Femi Adeyemi
suspects.motive: Heavily in debt; rumoured to have taken money to 'look away'.
suspects.alibi: Logged as the on-duty operator at the time of the blast.
suspects.note: Nervous, sweats, over-explains.

suspects.name: Grace Eze
suspects.motive: Could bury her own findings of theft by blaming a 'technical failure'.
suspects.alibi: At her hotel in Ikeja; CCTV pending.
suspects.note: Cool, asks more questions than she answers.

findings.heading: Relay bypass
findings.detail: Differential protection relay was placed in 'maintenance/inhibit' mode via the SCADA console, so the relay never tripped the breaker when the fault began.

findings.heading: Inhibit timestamp
findings.detail: The inhibit command was issued at 02:02 — twelve minutes before the blast. Not a standing test; a deliberate, fresh command.

findings.heading: Oil contamination
findings.detail: Insulating oil showed moisture above limits, consistent with overdue maintenance.

findings.heading: No physical tamper
findings.detail: No signs of forced entry to the transformer bay; everything was done from the console.

reports.title: SCADA Console Audit Log
reports.subtitle: Who issued what, and from where

findings.heading: Inhibit command origin
findings.detail: The 02:02 relay-inhibit command came from Console B, authenticated under operator Femi Adeyemi's login, which was active all night.

findings.heading: Shared session warning
findings.detail: Console B logged a 'session unlocked from idle' event at 02:00 with no password re-entry — the workstation had been left logged in and was used by whoever was physically there.

findings.heading: Chidinma's sign-out
findings.detail: Technician Okafor's badge logged an exit at 01:50; the badge does not reappear inside.

findings.heading: Earlier test
findings.detail: A routine relay test was logged at 22:30 and properly restored at 22:41.

findings.heading: Camera blind spot
findings.detail: Camera over Console B failed three days ago and was not repaired; Console A is covered.

findings.heading: Operator at Console A
findings.detail: Femi Adeyemi is on camera at Console A continuously from 01:30 to 02:20, never approaching Console B.

findings.heading: Second person
findings.detail: At 01:58 a figure in a contractor's hi-vis vest enters frame edge and moves toward the (off-camera) Console B; build and gait do not match Femi.

witnesses.name: Sgt. Ibrahim Lawal
witnesses.statement: Only two people badged in after midnight: the night-shift girl who left around ten-to-two, and the operator boy who never came out till the boom. Nobody else came through my gate.

witnesses.name: Blessing Umoh
witnesses.statement: There is a side door by the cable yard that the contractors prop open with a brick so they don't have to walk round. It doesn't badge anybody.

witnesses.name: Femi Adeyemi
witnesses.statement: I never touched the relay. I was at my desk. Somebody used my screen — I leave it open, everybody does, it's against the rules but everybody does it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Where were you at two this morning?

lines.speaker: Bakare
lines.text: Asleep. Alone. That post should have been mine — they gave it to a boy who can't tell a relay from a radio.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Could you inhibit a relay from home?

lines.speaker: Bakare
lines.text: Nobody can. SCADA only takes commands from inside that room. That's the whole point of the room.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who could reach Console B?

lines.speaker: Bakare
lines.text: Anyone standing in front of it, if the operator left it logged on — which Femi always does, the lazy one.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You sound like you know that console well.

lines.speaker: Bakare
lines.text: I supervised that room for nine years. I know everything except how to be at home asleep and in two places at once.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The inhibit command was under your login.

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: Because I never logged out! That's all it proves. I was at Console A the whole time — your own camera shows it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who walked in at 01:58?

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: A maintenance vest. I assumed it was one of Alhaji's boys doing oil checks, they come odd hours. I didn't look up properly.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Alhaji was in Abeokuta.

lines.speaker: Femi
lines.text: Then it wasn't his boy, was it? But the vest — anybody can wear that vest. They hang in the cable yard. Anybody who's worked here knows where.

timeline.event: Routine relay test completed and restored
Femi seen at Console A (camera)
Chidinma badges out of the building
Hi-vis figure enters, moves to Console B
Console B 'unlocked from idle', no password
Relay inhibit issued from Console B under Femi's login
Transformer T2 arcs and explodes
Bakare's phone connects to a mast near the substation

tools.name: Personnel & Badge Identity
tools.tagline: Match badges to people and credentials

rows.label: Note
rows.value: Privilege not needed if console already authenticated

results.note: Wide access; doesn't single anyone out.

rows.label: Result
rows.value: Vests unnumbered, stored in open rack at cable yard

results.note: Anyone could take one.

tools.name: Mast Triangulation
tools.tagline: Locate a handset by tower hand-offs

results.note: He arrives AFTER the blast — drawn by the alarm, like half the staff. Not before.
She is demonstrably leaving the area during the inhibit window.
Suggestive of a bribe — but the payer was 200km away that night.
Auditor was at her hotel; cleared.

rows.label: Record
rows.value: Grievance complaints only; no sabotage history

results.note: Angry, not criminal.

rows.value: Cadence matches enrolled profile: T. BAKARE

results.note: The person who typed the inhibit had Bakare's stored typing cadence.

explanation: Engr. Tunde Bakare did it. Chain: (r2) the inhibit was issued from Console B, which had been left logged in under Femi's name and was 'unlocked from idle' with no password at 02:00 — so the login proves nothing about identity; whoever was physically standing there did it. (r3) the camera shows Femi never left Console A and a hi-vis figure entered at 01:58 moving to the off-camera Console B — Femi is bodily ruled out. The decisive synthesis is (tool-crime) Console B's keystroke-cadence biometric matching Bakare's enrolled profile for the 02:00–02:03 inhibit, combined with witness w2's propped side door that lets someone enter without badging — explaining how Bakare got in past the gate (Sgt. Lawal only saw the front gate). Bakare wore a vest from the open rack he knew about from nine years supervising the room. Herrings: Femi's ₦400k from Danjuma Services looks like a bribe, but Danjuma (s3) has a wedding-full of witnesses 200km away (alibi confirmed) and the payment was 9 days prior. Femi's login on the inhibit command is the loudest false trail. The 02:18 triangulation putting Bakare 'at the scene' actually clears the timing fear — he's arriving after the blast like other staff — yet his keystroke cadence at 02:02 from inside is the real damning fact. Chidinma (s2) badged out and her phone proves she was a kilometre away and moving during the inhibit window. Grace (s5) was online at her hotel all night. The oil contamination and the earlier 22:41 relay test are pure noise.

title: The Benin Bronze Swap
brief: A 16th-century Benin bronze plaque, loaned to a private museum in Benin City for a homecoming exhibition, is discovered to be a resin forgery the morning after the gala. The real plaque was switched sometime during the event. Five people had the access and the knowledge to do it — and one of them needed the original to settle a very modern debt.

suspects.name: Dr. Osaze Igbinedion
suspects.motive: Custodian of the piece; in quiet financial trouble, his foundation underfunded.
suspects.alibi: Hosting the gala on the floor the entire evening; dozens saw him.
suspects.note: Charming, grief-stricken, helpful to a fault.

suspects.name: Aisha Bello
suspects.motive: Only person with the skill to cast a museum-grade resin copy.
suspects.alibi: In the conservation lab 'doing final checks' alone for part of the night.
suspects.note: Brilliant, terse, resents being the obvious suspect.

suspects.name: Chief Nosa Eweka
suspects.motive: Publicly demanded the bronze stay in Benin forever; a 'repatriation by theft' zealot.
suspects.alibi: At the high table all night, then driven home by his aide.
suspects.note: Booming, righteous, openly hostile to the foreign lender.

suspects.name: Ehi Aigbe
suspects.motive: Cash-strapped; handled the only crate that left the building that night.
suspects.alibi: Loading the deinstall crate at the dock 23:30–00:30.
suspects.note: Eager, defers to everyone, jingling keys.

suspects.name: Linda Okocha
suspects.motive: Could profit from a fraudulent claim, or be covering a prior switch.
suspects.alibi: Arrived only the next morning to inspect; flight records pending.
suspects.note: Clinical, asks for paperwork before answers.

findings.heading: Material
findings.detail: The object on display is pigmented resin with a bronze-powder surface coat. Not metal. A forgery.

findings.heading: Casting age
findings.detail: Resin curing markers indicate the copy was cast 5–8 weeks ago — long before the gala.

findings.heading: Mould source
findings.detail: Surface micro-detail shows the copy was moulded directly off the ORIGINAL, requiring extended unsupervised access to the real plaque weeks earlier.

findings.heading: Tool marks
findings.detail: Edge filing consistent with professional conservation tools.

findings.heading: Gala-night vault
findings.detail: The display vault was opened only twice on gala night: once by the curator for the unveiling, once by security at close. No covert opening.

findings.heading: Off-hours lab access
findings.detail: Six weeks ago, the plaque was signed out to the conservation lab for 'condition reporting' for nine days — far longer than a report needs.

findings.heading: Sign-out authoriser
findings.detail: That nine-day lab loan was authorised on the curator's account but the daily handling log was completed and signed by the conservator each day.

findings.heading: Crate scan
findings.detail: Gala-night deinstall crate was X-rayed at the dock; contents matched the manifest, no plaque-shaped metal object hidden.

findings.heading: Curator's foundation
findings.detail: Underfunded but no large inflows; his debts are old and static.

findings.heading: Anonymous escrow
findings.detail: A London antiquities broker placed $90,000 in escrow 'pending authentication of a West African bronze', released to a Nigerian account ten days ago — BEFORE the gala.

findings.heading: Receiving account
findings.detail: Escrow funds landed in a company, 'Edo Heritage Crafts Ltd', whose sole signatory is masked behind a nominee.

findings.heading: Crating firm cashflow
findings.detail: Ehi Aigbe's firm took a ₦2m short-term loan two weeks ago — for a truck repair, documented.

witnesses.name: Patience Idahosa
witnesses.statement: During those nine days the plaque was in the lab, the restorer kept the side bench under a dust sheet and asked me not to touch it. I thought she was just being careful, the way she is.

witnesses.name: Tony Osagie
witnesses.statement: I shot the plaque close up at the unveiling, around eight. Honestly it already looked a touch too even, too matte. I assumed it was the lighting.

witnesses.name: Chief Nosa Eweka
witnesses.statement: If I could keep that bronze in Benin by any means, God knows I would. But I never left that high table. Ask the governor — he was beside me the whole night.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You had the plaque in your lab for nine days.

lines.speaker: Aisha
lines.text: For a condition report. Yes. Standard.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Nine days is long for a report.

lines.speaker: Aisha
lines.text: There was active corrosion. I documented everything. Check the log — I signed every day.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Could you cast a resin copy from a piece like that?

lines.speaker: Aisha
lines.text: Technically, anyone with a mould could. The skill isn't the casting, it's getting the patina right — and getting it photographed first.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You said 'photographed'.

lines.speaker: Aisha
lines.text: To match the patina. From reference images. That's all I meant. I was describing a process, not confessing to one.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Your crate was the only thing that left the building.

lines.speaker: Ehi
lines.text: And you X-rayed it! Nothing metal, no plaque. I begged you to check, you checked.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The loan you took — two million.

lines.speaker: Ehi
lines.text: For my truck. I have the receipt, the mechanic, everything. I move boxes, oga. I wouldn't know a real bronze from a doorstop.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who told you which crate to load?

lines.speaker: Ehi
lines.text: The deinstall sheet. Madam restorer prepped it and the curator signed it. I just carry what the paper says to carry.

timeline.event: Resin copy cast (per curing markers)
Plaque signed out to lab for 9 days
$90k escrow released to 'Edo Heritage Crafts'
Plaque unveiled (already a forgery per photographer)
Ehi loads deinstall crate (X-rayed clean)
Chief Eweka leaves high table briefly (10 min)
Forgery discovered; insurer arrives

results.note: The escrow money flowed to the conservator, via a shell she set up before the loan even arrived.

rows.label: Result
rows.value: Only the registered foundation; no offshore links

results.note: Curator clear of the receiving account.

rows.label: Uploads
rows.value: 210 macro images of BB-114, all angles

results.note: Reference set needed to fake the patina — exactly the 'photographed first' she described.
Nothing art-related.
Eweka's single call is about his public campaign; Aisha's pattern is transactional.

rows.label: Flight
rows.value: Landed morning after gala; not in city before

results.note: Linda Okocha couldn't have made the swap.

rows.label: Flag
rows.value: Vocal repatriation activist; no theft record

results.note: Loud cause, clean record.

rows.label: Resin batch
rows.value: Same supplier lot as lab stock

results.note: The copy was made with the museum's own lab materials.

explanation: Aisha Bello, the conservator, did it. The whole crime predates the gala — that's the trick. Chain: (r1) the forgery was cast 5–8 weeks ago and moulded directly off the original, requiring extended unsupervised access; (r2) the original was signed out to her lab for an unnecessary nine days six weeks earlier — exactly that access — and the gala-night crate was X-rayed clean, proving nothing physically left that night; the swap had already happened. The decisive money link is (r3) the $90k escrow released BEFORE the gala to 'Edo Heritage Crafts Ltd', which (tool-id) KYC ties to A. Bello via a shell she incorporated 11 weeks ago. Corroborated by tool-phone (210 macro mould-prep images in her cloud during the loan) and tool-crime (copy made from the museum's own resin lot and lab silicone). Herrings, each a stronger surface suspect: Chief Eweka (s3) has the loudest motive ('keep it in Benin') and even leaves the high table for 10 minutes — but the plaque was already fake by unveiling at 20:00, so his absence is irrelevant; his single London call is about his public campaign. Ehi (s4) handled the only crate and took a suspicious loan, but the X-ray clears the crate and his loan is a documented truck repair. The curator (s1) authorised the loan and is in money trouble, but he has no offshore account and never called the broker; his sign-out authority is the misdirection. Linda (s5) only flew in the next morning. The everyone-was-watching-the-gala framing makes people hunt for a gala-night switch that never happened.

title: The Cocoa Ledger
brief: A farmers' cooperative in Ondo State that exports cocoa discovers ₦340 million missing across one season: phantom tonnage, ghost suppliers, and inflated freight. The board demands a culprit before the AGM. Several officers had their hands on the books — but only one engineered the gap, and the cleverest cover was making someone else look guilty.

suspects.name: Bayo Falade
suspects.motive: Controls disbursements; lavish lifestyle the salary can't explain.
suspects.alibi: N/A (financial crime over months).
suspects.note: Flashy, defensive, blames 'the system'.

suspects.name: Funmilayo Adeola
suspects.motive: Posted most of the suspicious entries; in the system daily.
suspects.alibi: N/A.
suspects.note: Meticulous, frightened, keeps printing screenshots.

suspects.name: Engr. Kola Ojo
suspects.motive: Approved the freight contracts; up for re-election, wants no scandal.
suspects.alibi: N/A.
suspects.note: Statesmanlike, deflects to 'due process'.

suspects.name: Ibrahim Sani
suspects.motive: Certifies tonnage; could pass phantom weights.
suspects.alibi: N/A.
suspects.note: Plain-spoken, irritated, points at the office staff.

suspects.name: Grace Mbah
suspects.motive: Could shape blame; paid by the chairman.
suspects.alibi: N/A.
suspects.note: Crisp, careful with wording, never volunteers.

findings.heading: Ghost suppliers
findings.detail: Eleven 'farmer-suppliers' were paid for cocoa with no weighbridge ticket. Bank accounts trace to three numbers.

findings.heading: Inflated freight
findings.detail: Freight invoices padded ~22%; the haulier confirms lower agreed rates.

findings.heading: Entry authorship
findings.detail: All eleven ghost-supplier entries were posted under the chief accountant's user ID, Funmilayo.

findings.heading: Approval override
findings.detail: Each ghost payment cleared a control because it carried a treasurer's 'pre-approved supplier' flag, which only the treasurer's role can set.

findings.heading: After-hours posting
findings.detail: Nine of the eleven ghost entries were posted between 21:00 and 23:00 from inside the office network.

findings.heading: Funmilayo's hours
findings.detail: Funmilayo's badge shows she leaves by 18:00 daily (school-run); she has no after-hours building access.

findings.heading: Shared terminal
findings.detail: The after-hours sessions came from the accounts terminal, where Funmilayo's password was saved/auto-filled — a known office habit.

findings.heading: Password resets
findings.detail: Funmilayo's password was reset by IT twice this season after lockouts.

findings.heading: Ghost account ownership
findings.detail: The three receiving accounts were opened with a BVN that links them all to one identity behind a trading name, 'Falade & Sons Produce'.

findings.heading: Freight kickback
findings.detail: The padded freight overage was split with the haulier; the haulier names the chairman's office as the contact who set rates.

findings.heading: Auditor fee
findings.detail: Grace Mbah's audit fee was standard and paid by cheque; no irregular inflows.

findings.heading: Weighbridge gratuities
findings.detail: Small cash 'dash' to the warehouse officer for fast clearances — petty, customary, ₦tens of thousands.

witnesses.name: Segun Aderibigbe
witnesses.statement: After everybody goes, sometimes Oga Treasurer comes back to 'finish reports'. He uses madam accountant's desk because his own computer is slow, he says. I lock up after him.

witnesses.name: Hauliers' rep (Musa)
witnesses.statement: The rate was set by the chairman's people. We padded and shared, yes. But the farmer payments? That's not us, we only carry the bags that exist.

witnesses.name: Funmilayo Adeola
witnesses.statement: Those entries are under my name but I never made them. I'm gone by six every day for my children. Pull the door logs — I am not in that building at night.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Eleven ghost suppliers cleared a control only your role can set.

lines.speaker: Bayo
lines.text: The 'pre-approved' flag? Anyone could have used my role if they had my login. Funmilayo posted those entries, not me. Her name is on every one.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: We didn't say the flag and the posting were the same person.

lines.speaker: Bayo
lines.text: Well — obviously the same session. You post and you approve, na one workflow.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Were you ever in the office at night this season?

lines.speaker: Bayo
lines.text: Maybe once or twice. My machine is slow. I'd use any free desk to finish. That's not a crime.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Whose desk?

lines.speaker: Bayo
lines.text: Whichever is free. I don't keep a register of chairs, Detective.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The ghost entries are under your user ID.

lines.speaker: Funmi
lines.text: Posted at night. I am never here at night. My password auto-fills on that terminal — everyone knows it, I begged IT to stop saving it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Who returns after hours?

lines.speaker: Funmi
lines.text: The treasurer. He sits at my desk, says his system is slow. Ask the cleaner who locks up.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Could you set a 'pre-approved supplier' flag?

lines.speaker: Funmi
lines.text: No! That control is the treasurer's role only. The system would block me. That's the one thing I couldn't have done even if I wanted to.

timeline.event: ₦340m leaks via ghosts + freight
Funmilayo badges OUT (school run)
Ghost entries posted from accounts terminal
Treasurer returns, uses accountant's desk
Freight padding set by chairman's office
Auditor signs interim accounts without flagging gap

tools.name: BVN / Identity Trace
tools.tagline: Tie accounts to a real person

results.note: All ghost money funnels to the treasurer's own BVN.
The accountant did not benefit.

tools.name: Device & Login Records
tools.tagline: Who was where at posting time

rows.label: Note
rows.value: His handset present during after-hours entries

results.note: He's physically there when the ghost entries are posted under her name.
Demonstrably absent when 'her' entries were posted.
Real freight kickback — but a SEPARATE, smaller scheme, not the ₦340m core.
Treasurer not part of the freight side.
The control on every ghost payment is one only the treasurer's role can apply.
Guilty of the freight scam, not the ledger gap.

explanation: Bayo Falade, the treasurer, engineered the ₦340m. The frame on the accountant is the whole misdirection. Chain: (r1) every ghost payment carried a 'pre-approved supplier' flag, and (tool-crime) that flag can ONLY be set by the treasurer's role — the accountant's role is system-blocked from it, so Funmilayo literally could not have completed these payments. (r2) the entries were posted after hours from the accounts terminal where Funmilayo's password auto-fills, but her badge shows she leaves at 18:00 and has no night access — confirmed by (tool-phone) her device being home on all nine posting nights, while Falade's handset was at the office on eight of nine. The money proof seals it: (r3 + tool-id) all three ghost accounts trace by BVN to Falade's own 'Falade & Sons Produce'. Herrings, each pointing at a different innocent: the entries posted under Funmilayo's ID make her the obvious patsy (she's the loud broken-alibi suspect, except her alibi turns out solid). The chairman (s3) genuinely ran a freight-padding kickback with the haulier — a real but separate, smaller crime (tool-call shows he's freight-only), so he looks dirty yet didn't touch the core ledger. The warehouse officer's petty 'dash' and the password resets are noise. Even Falade's own slip in interview — answering about a 'pre-approved flag and approval session' before the detective linked posting to approval — shows he knows the workflow that only his role enables. The chairman is guilty of something; the treasurer is guilty of this.

title: The Generator Room
brief: A wealthy importer is found dead in his Abuja home office at dawn, apparently from carbon-monoxide poisoning after running a generator too close to the house during a long blackout — a tragic, common accident. But the autopsy timing and a single moved object suggest he was already beyond saving before the generator ever ran. The household had every reason, and one of them had the opportunity nobody noticed.

suspects.name: Mrs. Halima Yusuf
suspects.motive: Stands to inherit; bitter divorce; prenup in dispute.
suspects.alibi: Says she slept in the guest wing; staff vaguely confirm.
suspects.note: Composed, almost rehearsed, grieves on cue.

suspects.name: Emeka Yusuf
suspects.motive: Cut from the will weeks ago; furious, indebted.
suspects.alibi: Clubbing in town; returned 'around 4 a.m.'.
suspects.note: Volatile, hungover, contradicts himself on times.

suspects.name: Sunday Okoro
suspects.motive: Owed months of back pay; knows the house's every habit.
suspects.alibi: In the boys' quarters; says he started the generator at 1 a.m. on the boss's order.
suspects.note: Deferential, careful, eyes down.

suspects.name: Dr. Ngozi Eke
suspects.motive: Manages his medications; named in the will; close to the wife.
suspects.alibi: At her own home across town; phone records pending.
suspects.note: Calm, clinical, answers in precise medical terms.

suspects.name: Tijani Bello
suspects.motive: A buy-out clause: the death lets him absorb the deceased's shares cheaply.
suspects.alibi: On a night flight from Lagos; boarding pass produced.
suspects.note: Brisk, all-business, faux-sympathetic.

reports.title: Post-Mortem — V. Yusuf
reports.subtitle: Cause and crucially, time of death

findings.heading: Carboxyhaemoglobin
findings.detail: Blood CO saturation is only 14% — elevated but NOT a fatal level. CO did not kill him.

findings.heading: True cause
findings.detail: Cause of death is a massive overdose of his cardiac sedative; lividity and stomach contents put death at roughly 23:00–00:00.

findings.heading: Time conflict
findings.detail: Death precedes the 01:00 generator start by an hour or more. The generator was staged AFTER death to fake an accident.

findings.heading: Old bruise
findings.detail: A healing bruise on the forearm, days old, unrelated.

findings.heading: Nightcap glass
findings.detail: His usual whisky glass contains a heavy concentration of the dissolved sedative — far beyond a therapeutic dose.

findings.heading: Pill organiser
findings.detail: The week's sedative compartments are EMPTY for the days ahead — pills removed to make up the lethal dose.

findings.heading: Moved generator
findings.detail: Scuff marks show the generator was repositioned to vent toward the office window after midnight; soot pattern is too fresh and localised for an all-night run.

findings.heading: Fingerprints on glass
findings.detail: Only the deceased's prints on the glass — wiped and re-placed in his hand, or he drank it himself unknowing.

findings.heading: Whisky brand
findings.detail: Premium single malt, his known nightly habit; nothing unusual about the bottle.

findings.heading: Office door
findings.detail: Smart lock shows the office opened at 22:40 by a fob, then not again until the body was found.

findings.heading: Fob owner
findings.detail: The 22:40 fob is the spare 'medical' fob issued to the physician for house calls — not the wife's, not the son's.

findings.heading: Son's return
findings.detail: Front gate logged Emeka's car at 04:10 — after death, after the generator was moved.

findings.heading: Wi-Fi
findings.detail: The wife's tablet streamed video in the guest wing 22:00–01:30 continuously.

witnesses.name: Amaka Nwosu
witnesses.statement: Oga always took his nightcap at ten-thirty, same glass, while he did his accounts. The doctor knew that better than anyone — she set up his whole evening routine.

witnesses.name: Sunday Okoro
witnesses.statement: Oga buzzed me at one and said put on the gen, the power had gone. So I did. He sounded — well, I assumed it was him on the intercom. I didn't go up.

witnesses.name: Emeka Yusuf
witnesses.statement: Yes I hated the will. Yes I came home drunk at four. But he was already gone by then — the gen was already running when I drove in. I didn't even go to his office.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You hold a fob to the house.

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: For house calls. He had a heart condition; I came when needed. I wasn't there last night.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: His sedative — who manages the dosing?

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: I prescribe and fill his organiser, yes. It's a delicate drug; too much is dangerous, which is exactly why I control it.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: When did you last see him?

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: Two days ago. He was alive and well when I left — and obviously alive at one when he asked Sunday for the generator.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: We never told you the generator was requested at one.

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: Sunday must have mentioned it. Or — the staff talk. It's a small house, Detective.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Walk me through last night.

lines.speaker: Emeka
lines.text: Club till late, home maybe three, maybe four. I don't know. I was wrecked.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The gate logs you at 04:10.

lines.speaker: Emeka
lines.text: Fine, four-ten. The gen was already roaring when I came in. I figured the old man was being the old man. I went straight to bed.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You were cut from the will.

lines.speaker: Emeka
lines.text: And I'm telling you he was dead before I got here. You can't pin a time on me that I wasn't even in the house for. Check the gate. Check it.

timeline.event: Victim takes his usual nightcap (routine)
Office opened by 'medical' fob
Actual time of death (sedative OD)
Sunday starts generator on intercom 'order'
Generator repositioned to vent at office window
Emeka's car logged at the gate
Body discovered

results.note: The only fob that opened the office that night belongs to the physician.

results.query: Who can access the locked drug supply?
results.note: Only she controls enough sedative to make a lethal dose.

rows.value: Moving away, toward her side of town

results.note: She claimed she was home all night. Her phone was AT the house during the death window.
He really was out until after death and the staging — his broken alibi is, ironically, sound for the crime.
The 'order' came from the office — but the man was dead by then. Someone used the intercom to stage the generator excuse.
Business partner was on a plane; cleared.
The physician quietly benefits AND would control a trust — overlooked beside the obvious wife/son.

rows.label: Last refill
rows.value: Dr. Eke, oversupplied by 3x normal, 5 days prior

results.note: She stockpiled exactly the surplus needed for a lethal dose, days before.

explanation: Dr. Ngozi Eke, the physician, murdered him. The 'generator accident' is a stage set. Chain: (r1) CO saturation is only 14% — non-fatal; he actually died of a sedative overdose at 23:00–00:00, BEFORE the 01:00 generator start, so the generator was repositioned afterward (r2) to fake CO poisoning. Means: (r2 + tool-crime) the lethal dose came from his own sedative, and only Eke fills his organiser and holds the spare stock — which she oversupplied 3x five days earlier ('travel buffer'). Opportunity: (r3 + tool-id) the office was opened at 22:40 by the spare 'medical' fob issued solely to her, with the wife's and son's fobs inactive all night; (tool-tri) her phone was at the estate from 22:10 to 23:30 — straddling the death window — though she claimed to be home. She then triggered the 01:00 intercom 'order' (tool-call) to plant the generator excuse and seed Sunday with the very detail she let slip in interview ('he asked Sunday for the generator at one' — never told to her). Herrings, each a louder suspect: Emeka (s2) has the broken, contradictory alibi and the rage of disinheritance, but triangulation puts him at the club until 03:50 and at the gate only at 04:10 — after death and staging, so he couldn't have done it. The wife (s1) inherits most and her alibi is shaky, but her tablet streamed continuously in the guest wing and her fob was inactive. Sunday (s3) started the generator but on a faked order. Tijani (s5) was airborne. The bruise, the wiped glass prints, the will's obvious beneficiaries — all draw the eye to wife and son, while the physician, the one person who controlled the drug, held the only active fob, and quietly stood to administer a trust, hides behind 'I wasn't there.'

title: The Influencer's Tax
brief: A rising Lagos lifestyle influencer is being bled dry by an anonymous extortionist who threatens to leak doctored 'scandal' footage unless paid in crypto. The blackmailer knows things only her inner circle could know — and the threats arrive in a voice and rhythm she swears she recognises. The catch: the most obvious insider has the cleanest alibi, and the money trail loops back to someone hiding in plain sight.

suspects.name: Zainab Lawal
suspects.motive: Underpaid, overworked, knows every secret and password.
suspects.alibi: Out of the country (Dubai) when two key threats were sent.
suspects.note: Loyal-seeming, exhausted, quick to tears.

suspects.name: Demola 'DJ Dex' Cole
suspects.motive: Bitter breakup; broke; has the original of the 'scandal' clip.
suspects.alibi: On tour, gigging on threat nights; club posters and crowds.
suspects.note: Smooth, performative, enjoys the attention.

suspects.name: Kelechi Obi
suspects.motive: Takes 30%; her income falls if the influencer's brand collapses — or rises if she controls a 'crisis'.
suspects.alibi: With the influencer at events on several threat nights.
suspects.note: Protective, controlling, finishes the client's sentences.

suspects.name: Sandra Effiong
suspects.motive: Envious of the rise; her own numbers stalled; needs a comeback.
suspects.alibi: Posting live content during some threats.
suspects.note: Bubbly, oversharing, never off her phone.

suspects.name: Bright Nwachukwu
suspects.motive: Has raw footage and the editing skill to fabricate a 'scandal' clip.
suspects.alibi: Freelance, often alone editing at night.
suspects.note: Quiet, technical, talks in frame rates and codecs.

findings.heading: Voice notes
findings.detail: Three threats were voice notes using a pitch-shifter, but residual cadence and a stutter on 'p' sounds were preserved under the filter.

findings.heading: Doctored clip
findings.detail: The 'scandal' video is a deepfake-lite composite: real party footage with a fabricated audio bed. Built in a pro editor with frame-accurate cuts.

findings.heading: Sender metadata
findings.detail: Messages routed through a VPN, but two early ones leaked a device model and a Lagos ISP before the VPN was configured.

findings.heading: Insider knowledge
findings.detail: Threats reference a private hospital visit and a contract figure known to perhaps four people.

findings.heading: Wallet hops
findings.detail: Funds bounced through three mixer-style wallets before consolidating.

findings.heading: Cash-out point
findings.detail: Final consolidation cashed out via a P2P merchant to a local bank account during business hours.

findings.heading: P2P merchant KYC
findings.detail: The P2P trade required ID; the buyer used a verified account under a trading alias.

findings.heading: Amounts
findings.detail: Payments escalated; classic extortion ramp.

reports.title: Device Model & Network Correlation
reports.subtitle: Matching the leak to a phone

findings.heading: Device model
findings.detail: Pre-VPN leak shows a specific phone model + an unusual custom keyboard app fingerprint.

findings.heading: ISP subscriber zone
findings.detail: The Lagos ISP cell serves the mainland district where two suspects live; not the island where the influencer's circle mostly resides.

findings.heading: Active during travel
findings.detail: Threats continued on nights the assistant was provably in Dubai (different country, different SIM, roaming logs).

findings.heading: Editing software licence
findings.detail: The clip's editor watermark traces to a widely pirated build; no unique owner.

witnesses.name: Influencer (the victim, Adaeze)
witnesses.statement: The voice — even bent up like that — there's a little catch on 'p' words. Two people I know talk like that. One is my editor. The other is my best friend when she's nervous.

witnesses.name: Uche (the influencer's brother)
witnesses.statement: Only four of us knew about the hospital thing: me, Adaeze, her manager, and the editor who filmed the 'get-well' content. Sandra wasn't told. Zainab was, but Zainab was in Dubai.

witnesses.name: P2P crypto merchant
witnesses.statement: Yeah I bought that USDT batch. The seller's verified account trades under a craft-business name. Regular guy, mainland, does it on the side. I've traded with that alias before.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Adaeze says the voice could be yours when you're nervous.

lines.speaker: Sandra
lines.text: Because I stammer a little? Half of Lagos stammers. I'd never do this to her, we came up together.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Did you know about her hospital visit?

lines.speaker: Sandra
lines.text: No! I found out when everyone did. The blackmailer obviously knew, so it can't be me — I didn't even know.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Where were you on the threat nights?

lines.speaker: Sandra
lines.text: Live on my page, half the time. You can see the timestamps. I overshare for a living, Detective, I'm the worst person to run a secret operation.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You could build that doctored clip.

lines.speaker: Bright
lines.text: Anyone with a cracked editor could. The cuts aren't even that clean — I'd be embarrassed to send that.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You knew about the hospital visit.

lines.speaker: Bright
lines.text: I filmed the get-well posts, so yes, I was one of the four. I'm not hiding that. Being in the room isn't a crime.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Do you trade crypto?

lines.speaker: Bright
lines.text: A little, side income, like everybody. Under a craft name for my video gear business. That's — that's not unusual.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: We hadn't mentioned a craft-business trading name.

lines.speaker: Bright
lines.text: You — the merchant must have said it. People talk. It's a small scene.

timeline.event: First threat (pre-VPN device leak)
Sandra live-posting during a threat
Two threats sent while Zainab is in Dubai
Doctored clip delivered
Crypto cashed out via P2P, business hours
Manager seen with victim at event during a threat

tools.name: Device Fingerprint Match
tools.tagline: Tie the leaked model to an owner

results.query: Match leaked phone model + keyboard fingerprint

rows.label: Keyboard app
rows.value: same niche editor-shortcut keyboard he uses

rows.label: Others
rows.value: No other suspect runs that combo

results.note: The pre-VPN slip fingerprints the editor's device.
Manager's tech doesn't fit the leak.
Under the filter it's a male voice with the editor's stutter — not the best friend's.
The ex doesn't match the voice.
The ransom cashed out to the editor's own KYC'd trading account.

rows.label: Location
rows.value: Dubai, roaming — two threats sent while abroad

results.note: Assistant physically abroad; cleared as sender.

tools.name: Records Cross-Check
tools.tagline: Who knew, who could fake it

results.query: Who knew the hospital + contract details?
results.note: Sandra literally didn't have the insider facts — rules her out despite the 'voice'.

results.query: DJ Dex tour dates vs threats

rows.label: Gigs
rows.value: On stage on multiple threat nights, crowds + posters

results.note: Ex-boyfriend alibi confirmed.

explanation: Bright Nwachukwu, the videographer, is the blackmailer. It takes three threads braided together. Thread one (r1 voice notes + tool-phone): under the pitch-shifter the voice is MALE with a plosive 'p' stutter — which matches Bright and excludes Sandra (female register, no match) and DJ Dex (no stutter). Thread two (r1/r3 device leak + tool-id): two early pre-VPN messages leaked a phone model and a niche editor-shortcut keyboard fingerprint that match Bright's retainer phone and no one else's, on a mainland ISP cell (he lives mainland; the circle is island). Thread three (r2 + tool-call): the crypto cashed out to a P2P seller whose KYC alias — a craft/gear trading name — resolves to B. Nwachukwu, cashing out in business hours at a mainland branch. Add the closed-set fact (witness w2 + tool-crime): only four people knew the hospital and contract details, and Bright was one of them. He even self-incriminates in interview, naming the 'craft-business trading name' before the detective mentions it. Herrings, each a more obvious suspect: Sandra (s4) is fingered by the victim's 'nervous voice' instinct and her stalled-career envy — but she wasn't told the insider facts and the de-pitched voice is male. Zainab (s1) knows every secret and is underpaid, the classic insider — but she was provably in Dubai (roaming logs) while threats kept coming. DJ Dex (s2) holds the original clip and is a bitter ex — but he was on stage before crowds on threat nights and his voice doesn't match. The manager (s3) has the cynical 'manufacture a crisis' motive and shaky event-night alibis, but her device and ISP don't fit the leak. The mixer wallets, the pirated-editor watermark, and the deepfake clip's craftsmanship are all designed to make you chase technical sophistication instead of the boring KYC slip and the stutter the filter couldn't hide.
